title: Dynamics of repeated BEC formation and extraction in dimple traps
authors: Kyrylo Kovalchuk, Dominik Pfeiffer, Ludwig Lind, Mark Edwards, Alexander Yakimenko, Gerhard Birkl
abstract: We investigate repeated Bose-Einstein-condensate (BEC) formation and extraction in a dimple trap embedded in a reservoir of thermal atoms using a kinetic model. The model includes pulsed extraction, evaporation, three-body losses, and thermal-atom replenishment. Three extraction protocols are compared: extraction of all atoms from the dimple (BEC and thermal atoms), full and partial extractions of the BEC, but not of the thermal atoms. Residual atoms in the dimple after extraction seed subsequent Bose-stimulated growth and reduce the recovery time between extractions, but also enhance density-dependent losses. For all protocols, repeated extraction of BECs can be achieved without replenishment, but the number of BEC formations is limited by reservoir depletion and heating. With continuous replenishment, the system can reach a periodic steady-state regime, after an initial transient period, controlled by the externally imposed rates of extraction pulses and thermal-atom input. Within the explored parameter range, partial BEC extraction gives the highest efficiency, particularly for short extraction periods and high input rates. These results identify seeding by residual populations of BECs and thermal atoms as a kinetic mechanism for improving repeated condensate production in dimple traps.
summary: The paper models the repeated formation and extraction of BECs from a dimple trap coupled to a thermal reservoir. By comparing different clearance protocols (e.g., partial vs. full removal), it determines that retaining residual atoms is key for efficient cycling. The results establish optimal operating regimes, particularly when continuous external atom replenishment is applied.
main_problem: The study investigates the dynamics of repeatedly forming and extracting Bose-Einstein condensates (BECs) from a localized dimple trap coupled to a thermal atom reservoir.
main_result: Partial BEC extraction protocols yield the highest efficiency for repeated condensate production, especially when combined with continuous external replenishment, establishing kinetic pathways for high-duty-cycle operation.
method: The research employs a detailed kinetic model that tracks the evolution of condensate and non-condensate populations alongside thermal reservoir dynamics.
model_or_system: The system consists of atoms confined in a dimple trap embedded within a larger, thermal atomic reservoir. The model incorporates pulsed extraction, evaporation, three-body losses, and external atom replenishment rates.
key_observables: Condensate fraction ($f_0$), non-condensed dimple population ($f_{nc}$), cumulative extracted particle number, and the system's steady-state efficiency ($\eta$).
important_parameters: Extraction period ($T_{extr}$), external replenishment rate ($\dot{N}_{in}$), and the relative strength of three-body losses versus Bose-stimulated growth.
main_assumptions: The thermal cloud is assumed to remain close to equilibrium, and the reservoir size is large compared to the dimple trap dimensions. The model treats extraction/loading as short processes relative to collisional relaxation times.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the time evolution of condensate fractions under different clearance protocols (Full, BEC, Partial) both with and without external replenishment, mapping out optimal operating regimes via efficiency heatmaps.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the kinetic model for the coupled system; it then analyzes cyclic dynamics in the absence of replenishment to compare extraction strategies; finally, it introduces continuous replenishment to map out high-efficiency, periodic steady-state operation.
why_interesting: This work provides a detailed kinetic framework for understanding non-equilibrium cycle dynamics in ultracold atomic systems, which is crucial for designing practical quantum simulation or atom-based quantum memory devices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. (a) Schematic of the trap geometry: a localized dimple potential (blue) is embedded in a harmonic thermal reservoir (red). The reservoir density is represented by an ellipsoidal isodensity surface for initial particle number Nr0 = 3×105 and temperature Tr0 = 150 nK. (b) Axial cross section of the trapping potential. Reservoir states occupy the energy range 0 < E < εt, whereas dimple states lie in the interval −εd < E < 0. (c) Collision processes included in the kinetic model: solid red arrows denote reservoir atoms and dashed blue arrows denote dimple atoms.

Figure 2. (a) Time evolution of the condensate fraction f0(t) (solid) and the noncondensed dimple fraction fnc(t) (dotted). Red and black curves correspond to calculations with and without three-body losses. Evaporation is suppressed by tak- ing εt →∞. (b) Energy distribution f( ˜E) of noncondensed atoms in the dimple at selected times for the calculation with three-body losses. The inset resolves the low-energy part of the distribution.

Figure 3. Repeated condensate extraction without external replenishment. Left panels show the condensate fraction f0(t) and noncondensed dimple fraction fnc(t). Right panels show the cumulative particle balance. The reservoir depth is εt = kB × 750 nK. The critical temperature in the absence of the dimple is Tc = 130 nK. (a),(b) Full-Clearance protocol. (c),(d) BEC-Clearance protocol. (e),(f) Partial-BEC-Clearance protocol.

Figure 4. Reservoir temperature as a function of time for the extraction protocols shown in Fig. 3. Condensate formation initially heats the reservoir, whereas subsequent evaporation from the finite-depth trap gradually lowers the temperature.

Figure 5. Fixed-period extraction without external replenish- ment. After the first extraction, subsequent extraction pulses are applied with period Textr = 2 s. Solid blue curves denote the condensate fraction f0(t), and dotted red curves denote the noncondensed dimple fraction fnc(t). (a) Partial-BEC- Clearance protocol with Γ0 = 15 s−1 and Γ = 0 . (b) Modified protocol including partial noncondensed-atom clearance with Γ0 = 15 s−1 and Γ = 20 s−1.
